parts.text:
The air over the field is not merely foul; it is heavy, a thick, cloying vapor that coats the back of the throat with the metallic tang of old blood and the sweet, dizzying rot of sun-scorched meat. Silence reigns here, broken only by the dry rustle of tattered banners and the rhythmic, sickening crunch of crows working through ribcages. What remains of the fallen has been stripped of dignity; the earth has begun to reclaim them, turning the soil into a dark, greasy mire where leather boots and shattered bone sink into a single, indistinguishable sludge. 

There is no glory left in the wreckage—only the stark, clinical reality of what happens when iron meets marrow. Limbs lie scattered like discarded kindling, drained of color and whittled down by scavengers until only the jagged, yellowed splinters of the humerus remain. Faces that once held conviction are now hollowed masks of grey parchment, their expressions frozen in a final, frantic gasp while the white blossoms of maggots churn rhythmically beneath the skin. To walk here is to feel the persistent, unsettling stickiness of the mud against your soles and to realize that the very ground beneath you is no longer made of stone, but of the cooling, quiet remains of ten thousand wasted lives.
